Predictors of Religiosity and Spirituality

Rachel Chen, Topher Hanagan, Jakob Hildebrandt

2026-04-20

Abstract

The level of one’s religiosity and spirituality are often associated with one’s other beliefs and habits regarding issues considered related in nature to faith, including political beliefs, family relationships, church attendance and prayer habits, among others. After some preliminary exploratory data analysis to get a feel for the spread of our data, we analyzed the group differences between faith traditions, the relationship between spirituality and religiosity, behavioral and political belief predictors of religiosity and spirituality, finally culminating in the complete models that predict the spirituality and religiosity of individuals based on most available factors. The complete models were able account for more of the variance of the religiosity of an individual, with an \(R^2\) of .71, than for the spirituality of an individual, which had an \(R^2\) of .41.

Introduction

The dataset that we’re working with comes from the PEW Research Center. The data comes from a survey of the American public about religious affiliation, conducted with the intention of tracking changes in U.S. religious affiliation. The survey was intended to get responses from as many Americans as possible, offering surveys in English and Spanish in all 50 states, with online, paper and phone format options. The data contains information about the religious, political, spiritual and social background of those surveyed. After trimming down our dataset to include only those with useful information and those without all NA values, we were left with 93 columns. Our driving question is twofold: 1) How well can we predict religiosity and spirituality based on factors including church attendance, family relationships, marriage, political opinions, religious affiliation and devotion, etc.? 2) Which factors are most effective in predicting religiosity/spirituality of individuals? Does this vary by religious affiliation? As one of the premier immigration destinations in the world, an analysis of the religious landscape in the United States provides key information on how

In our dataset, many variables were unusable due to lack of data or relevance to our research question. We narrowed our dataset down to 93 variables, of which the 28 most important were related to religious affiliation, current religion, level of religiosity and spirituality on a scale of 1-5, frequency of church service attendance, belief in God, Heaven and Hell, frequency of prayer, reading of scripture and attendance of religious services growing up, sharing beliefs with others, support of legalization of abortion, the religion of the respondents’ family, marital status, support of prayer in public schools, birth decade, race and hispanic status, educational attainment, current school enrollment and employment status, immigration and voting status, political party affiliation and views, income bracket and gender. Many other variables exist and are used in this paper, but these are a sampling of many that are related to the question of religiosity and spirituality.

Exploratory Data Analysis

First, we turned the current religion variable into a factor so that we can create “bins” of how many people fall into each religious tradition.

Taking a quick

cor(data$SPIRPER, data$RELPER)
## [1] 0.4362961

From there, we were able to look at the relationship between some of the other variables and religion.

First, before trying to plot the data, we noticed that there was a much larger number of those who identified as Catholic, Protestant or Unaffiliated than anything else, so we split the histogram facet_wrap into two to account for this (then split it again to account for the much larger prevalence of Mormon, Jewish and Other Faith than all the other religious identities combined) so that the spread of each religion across the different values would be clear. This splitting up of the religious categories will prove useful throughout the duration of the EDA section.

Looking at the correlation between each specific faith and personal spirituality (1=most spiritual, 4= least spiritual, 5=don’t know/refused), we notice a few trends: most faiths seemed to have the largest spike at either 1 or 2. Interestingly, the Unaffiliated category also has the largest spikes around 1 and 2, and not 4. This could be that many people who identified as Unaffiliated, although they did not profess a specific religion, still considered themselves spiritual (spiritual but not religious).

The spread for religiosity is shifted more to the higher (less religious) values than spirituality: there were only two religions where the plurality (largest spike) was 1 (most religious).

Statistical Analysis

To formally investigate the relationships suggested by the exploratory data analysis, we apply several statistical methods, including analysis of variance (ANOVA), correlation analysis, simple linear regression, and multiple linear regression. These methods allow us to test for group differences, measure associations between variables, and identify key predictors of religiosity.

Section 1: Group Differences

We begin by examining whether religiosity differs across religious traditions.

Hypothesis Test:

\(H_0\): All religious groups have the same mean religiosity.

\(H_a\): At least one group’s mean is different.

##                Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)    
## CURREL         13  17394  1338.0    2247 <2e-16 ***
## Residuals   36590  21785     0.6                   
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
Summary Statistics by Religious Group
Current Religion Mean Religiosity SD Sample Size
JW 1.380531 0.6313051 113
Mormon 1.463588 0.6669679 563
Protestant 1.938123 0.7856999 14949
Muslim 2.011029 0.8823127 272
Catholic 2.063366 0.7434991 6928
Orthodox 2.121076 0.7761389 223
Hindu 2.207317 0.7516698 246
Other Christian 2.388393 0.9592828 224
Other Religion 2.646465 1.0332545 99
Buddhist 2.651163 0.8907355 344
Uninterpretable 2.720109 0.9795890 368
Jewish 2.742243 0.8841662 838
Other Faith 3.047809 0.9327656 753
Unaffiliated 3.478285 0.7309605 10684

The ANOVA results indicate that we reject the null hypothesis, as the p-value is less than 0.05. This suggests that religiosity differs significantly across religious traditions.

To further examine these differences, we computed the mean religiosity score for each group. Religiosity is measured on a scale from 1 (very religious) to 4 (not at all religious), excluding responses coded as 5 (don’t know/refused). Because lower values indicate higher religiosity, groups with lower mean scores are more religious on average. The results show that Protestants have lower mean religiosity scores than Catholics and the unaffiliated, indicating higher average religiosity, while the unaffiliated group reports the highest mean scores, indicating the lowest levels of religiosity.

Protestants, Catholics, and the unaffiliated also have the largest sample sizes, making these estimates more reliable. The standard deviations, which range approximately from 0.5 to 1, suggest a moderate level of variation in religiosity within each group.

tukey_result <- TukeyHSD(anova_model)
tukey_result
##   Tukey multiple comparisons of means
##     95% family-wise confidence level
##

Tukey post-hoc tests reveal that most religious groups differ significantly in mean religiosity. In particular, the unaffiliated group exhibits substantially lower religiosity compared to all religious traditions, while Protestant respondents fall near the middle of the distribution. Jewish, Buddhist, and Other Faith groups also show significantly higher differences relative to Protestants. However, some comparisons, such as between Muslim and Protestant respondents, are not statistically significant, suggesting similar levels of religiosity between these groups.

Check Assumptions for ANOVA:

  1. Independence

  2. Normality within groups

  3. Common variance

To assess the assumptions of ANOVA, several diagnostic plots were examined. The residuals versus observation order plot shows no clear pattern, suggesting that the independence assumption is reasonably satisfied. The Q-Q plots across religious groups indicate that residuals are approximately normally distributed within each group, with only minor deviations in the tails, which is expected given the large sample sizes. Finally, the residuals versus fitted values plot shows a relatively constant spread of residuals, suggesting that the assumption of equal variances across groups is reasonably met. Overall, the diagnostic checks indicate that the assumptions required for ANOVA are adequately satisfied for this analysis.

Section 2: Spirituality vs Religiosity

Next, we examine the relationship between the spirituality and religiosity variables, as the exploratory data analysis suggested that even unaffiliated individuals report relatively high levels of spirituality.

## [1] 0.561914
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = RELPER ~ SPIRPER, data = data_no5)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -2.7564 -0.4704 -0.1134  0.5296  2.1726 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 1.184407   0.010869   109.0   <2e-16 ***
## SPIRPER     0.642997   0.004959   129.7   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.8557 on 36437 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.3157, Adjusted R-squared:  0.3157 
## F-statistic: 1.681e+04 on 1 and 36437 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

A correlation analysis was conducted between religiosity and spirituality, excluding missing responses. The result is 0.562, indicating a moderate positive relationship between the two variables. This suggests that higher levels of spirituality are associated with higher religiosity scores. Given that higher religiosity scores correspond to lower levels of religiosity (1 = very religious, 4 = not at all religious), this indicates that higher spirituality is associated with lower reported religiosity.

A simple linear regression further examined this relationship. The results show that spirituality is a statistically significant predictor of religiosity (p < 0.05). The coefficient indicates that a one-unit increase in spirituality is associated with an increase in religiosity score. The model explains approximately 31.57% (\(R^2\) = 0.3157) of the variation in religiosity, suggesting a moderate relationship.

As spirituality increases, religiosity score increased, indicating lower religiosity. A jittered scatterplot with a fitted linear regression line was used to visualize the relationship between spirituality and religiosity. Jittering was applied to help with data visualization due to the discrete nature of the 1–4 response scale.

We want to check the assumptions for linear model:

m1 <- augment(model1)

ggplot(data = m1, aes(x = .resid)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = ..density..), bins = 10) +
  stat_function(
    fun = dnorm,
    args = list(
      mean = mean(m1$.resid),
      sd = sd(m1$.resid)
    ),
    color = "blue",
    linewidth = 1.5
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Histogram of Residuals",
    x = "Residuals",
    y = "Density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(model1, aes(sample = .resid)) +
  stat_qq() +
  stat_qq_line() +
  labs(title = "Q-Q Plot of Residuals") +
  theme_minimal()

To assess the normality assumption of the linear model, both a histogram of residuals and a Q-Q plot were examined. The histogram shows that the residuals are approximately bell-shaped and centered around zero, with only minor deviations from normality. The Q-Q plot further indicates that most points lie close to the reference line, with slight deviations in the tails. Given the large sample size, these deviations are not concerning, and the normality assumption is considered reasonably satisfied.

Section 3: Behavioral Predictors

While spirituality captures internal beliefs, religiosity may also be influenced by observable behaviors such as attendance and prayer. We therefore examine whether these behavioral variables are significant predictors of religiosity.

We focused on four variables here, including frequency of religious service attendance, prayer, scripture reading, and sharing of religious views. We test whether these behavioral factors significantly predict religiosity.

Hypothesis Test:

\(H_0\): That none of these behaviors are associated with religiosity.

\(H_a\): That at least one behavioral variable significantly predicts religiosity.

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = RELPER ~ ATTNDPERRLS + PRAY + PRAC_B + PRAC_D, data = no99)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.61517 -0.40885 -0.05211  0.40467  2.84929 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  0.705294   0.012384  56.953   <2e-16 ***
## ATTNDPERRLS  0.215591   0.003086  69.868   <2e-16 ***
## PRAY         0.186483   0.002190  85.169   <2e-16 ***
## PRAC_B       0.063182   0.003543  17.833   <2e-16 ***
## PRAC_D      -0.004961   0.003687  -1.345    0.178    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.6659 on 35842 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.5856, Adjusted R-squared:  0.5855 
## F-statistic: 1.266e+04 on 4 and 35842 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

The results from the full model indicate that attendance, prayer frequency, and scripture reading are statistically significant predictors of religiosity (p < 0.05). Given the coding of religiosity (higher values indicate lower religiosity), these results suggest that more frequent religious practices are associated with higher levels of religiosity (i.e., more religious behavior). In contrast, PRAC_D is not statistically significant, suggesting that sharing religious views with others does not have a measurable relationship with religiosity in this model.

The model explains approximately 58.56% (\(R^2\) = 0.5856) of the variation in religiosity, indicating a relatively strong relationship between behavioral practices and religiosity.

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = RELPER ~ ATTNDPERRLS + PRAY + PRAC_B + PRAC_D, data = protestant_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -1.8208 -0.4932 -0.0820  0.3761  2.6364 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 1.054964   0.016932  62.307  < 2e-16 ***
## ATTNDPERRLS 0.119634   0.004542  26.341  < 2e-16 ***
## PRAY        0.102805   0.004215  24.391  < 2e-16 ***
## PRAC_B      0.067325   0.005328  12.637  < 2e-16 ***
## PRAC_D      0.018922   0.005331   3.549 0.000387 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.669 on 14560 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.2734, Adjusted R-squared:  0.2732 
## F-statistic:  1370 on 4 and 14560 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

The results from the Protestant model indicate that attendance (ATTNDPERRLS), prayer frequency (PRAY), scripture reading (PRAC_B), and sharing religious views (PRAC_D) are statistically significant predictors of religiosity (p < 0.05). Given the coding of religiosity, where higher values indicate lower levels of religiosity, these results suggest that more frequent religious practices are associated with lower religiosity scores, indicating higher levels of religious engagement.

The model explains approximately 27.34% (\(R^2\) = 0.2734) of the variation in religiosity, suggesting a moderate relationship between behavioral practices and religiosity within the Protestant subsample.

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = RELPER ~ ATTNDPERRLS + PRAY + PRAC_B + PRAC_D, data = catholic_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -2.1253 -0.3578 -0.0318  0.3484  2.8866 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept) 0.679348   0.025274  26.879  < 2e-16 ***
## ATTNDPERRLS 0.174389   0.005501  31.700  < 2e-16 ***
## PRAY        0.129122   0.004237  30.475  < 2e-16 ***
## PRAC_B      0.067755   0.006135  11.044  < 2e-16 ***
## PRAC_D      0.031407   0.007179   4.375 1.23e-05 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.5383 on 6809 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.4764, Adjusted R-squared:  0.4761 
## F-statistic:  1549 on 4 and 6809 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

The results from the Catholic model indicate that attendance (ATTNDPERRLS), prayer frequency (PRAY), scripture reading (PRAC_B), and sharing religious views (PRAC_D) are statistically significant predictors of religiosity (p < 0.05). Given the coding of religiosity, where higher values indicate lower levels of religiosity, these results suggest that more frequent religious practices are associated with lower religiosity scores, indicating higher levels of religious engagement.

The model explains approximately 47.64% (\(R^2\) = 0.4764) of the variation in religiosity, suggesting a relatively strong relationship between behavioral practices and religiosity within the Catholic subsample.

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = RELPER ~ ATTNDPERRLS + PRAY + PRAC_B + PRAC_D, data = muslim_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -1.90472 -0.51176 -0.07219  0.41528  2.37060 
## 
## Coefficients:
##             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  0.85483    0.13110   6.521 3.54e-10 ***
## ATTNDPERRLS  0.13021    0.02980   4.369 1.79e-05 ***
## PRAY         0.16148    0.02480   6.512 3.72e-10 ***
## PRAC_B       0.06696    0.03438   1.948   0.0525 .  
## PRAC_D       0.02527    0.03519   0.718   0.4732    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.6871 on 264 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.4063, Adjusted R-squared:  0.3973 
## F-statistic: 45.16 on 4 and 264 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

The results from the Muslim model indicate that attendance (ATTNDPERRLS), prayer frequency (PRAY), and scripture reading (PRAC_B) are statistically significant predictors of religiosity (p < 0.05). Given the coding of religiosity, where higher values indicate lower levels of religiosity, these results suggest that more frequent religious practices are associated with lower religiosity scores, indicating higher levels of religious engagement. In contrast, PRAC_D is not statistically significant, suggesting that sharing religious views with others does not have a measurable relationship with religiosity in this model.

The model explains approximately 40.63% (\(R^2\) = 0.4063) of the variation in religiosity, suggesting a moderate to relatively strong relationship between behavioral practices and religiosity within the Muslim subsample.

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = RELPER ~ ATTNDPERRLS + PRAY + PRAC_B + PRAC_D, data = unaffil_data)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -2.8749 -0.3659  0.1711  0.3101  2.5644 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  0.818845   0.044116  18.561  < 2e-16 ***
## ATTNDPERRLS  0.227620   0.007980  28.524  < 2e-16 ***
## PRAY         0.161981   0.003658  44.286  < 2e-16 ***
## PRAC_B       0.125079   0.008699  14.379  < 2e-16 ***
## PRAC_D      -0.022988   0.006884  -3.339 0.000843 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.5709 on 10515 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.3884, Adjusted R-squared:  0.3881 
## F-statistic:  1669 on 4 and 10515 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

The results from the Unaffiliated model indicate that some behavioral variables are statistically significant predictors of religiosity (p < 0.05). Given the coding of religiosity, where higher values indicate lower levels of religiosity, these results suggest that more frequent religious practices are associated with lower religiosity scores, indicating higher levels of religious engagement.

The model explains approximately 38.84% (\(R^2\) = 0.3884) of the variation in religiosity, suggesting a moderate relationship between behavioral practices and religiosity within the Unaffiliated subsample.

models <- list(
  Overall = model2,
  Protestant = model_protestant,
  Catholic = model_catholic,
  Muslim = model_muslim,
  Unaffiliated = model_unaffil
)

coef_df <- bind_rows(lapply(names(models), function(name) {
  tidy(models[[name]], conf.int = TRUE) |> 
    filter(term != "(Intercept)") |> 
    mutate(Group = name)
})) |>
  mutate(term = recode(term,
    ATTNDPERRLS = "Attendance",
    PRAY = "Prayer",
    PRAC_B = "Scripture Reading",
    PRAC_D = "Sharing Beliefs"
  ))

ggplot(coef_df, aes(x = estimate, y = term, color = Group)) +
  geom_point(position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)) +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = conf.low, xmax = conf.high),
                 position = position_dodge(width = 0.5), height = 0.2) +
  labs(
    title = "Behavioral Predictors of Religiosity",
    x = "Effect on Religiosity",
    y = "Behavioral Variables"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
## `height` was translated to `width`.

The coefficient plot compares the effects of behavioral predictors on religiosity across different religious groups. Each point represents the estimated effect size, with horizontal bars indicating 95% confidence intervals. Across all models, religious service attendance and prayer frequency consistently show strong positive associations with religiosity, indicating that more frequent participation in these behaviors is linked to higher levels of religiosity. Scripture reading also demonstrates a positive but smaller effect across groups. In contrast, sharing religious beliefs exhibits weaker and less consistent effects, with some confidence intervals crossing zero, suggesting limited predictive power. Overall, the results indicate that while core devotional practices are strong and stable predictors of religiosity across groups, the influence of interpersonal religious expression varies considerably.

Section 4: Political Predictors of Religiosity

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = RELPER ~ ABRTLGL + GAYMARR + QB2A + CHNG_C, data = data_clean)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -2.1085 -0.6519 -0.1085  0.8915  3.7058 
## 
## Coefficients:
##               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  3.6830172  0.0156302 235.634   <2e-16 ***
## ABRTLGL     -0.2550419  0.0065717 -38.809   <2e-16 ***
## GAYMARR     -0.3135173  0.0077475 -40.467   <2e-16 ***
## QB2A        -0.0049037  0.0166560  -0.294   0.7684    
## CHNG_C      -0.0010580  0.0004984  -2.123   0.0338 *  
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.921 on 35221 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.2378, Adjusted R-squared:  0.2377 
## F-statistic:  2748 on 4 and 35221 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = SPIRPER ~ ABRTLGL + GAYMARR + QB2A + CHNG_C, data = data_clean)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -1.3393 -0.6044 -0.1779  0.6618  3.5559 
## 
## Coefficients:
##               Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  2.683e+00  1.527e-02 175.670  < 2e-16 ***
## ABRTLGL     -1.603e-01  6.420e-03 -24.966  < 2e-16 ***
## GAYMARR     -1.145e-01  7.569e-03 -15.133  < 2e-16 ***
## QB2A        -6.954e-02  1.627e-02  -4.274 1.93e-05 ***
## CHNG_C       1.156e-05  4.870e-04   0.024    0.981    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.8998 on 35221 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.08104,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.08094 
## F-statistic: 776.5 on 4 and 35221 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

It would appear that removal of the 99s which represent those who did not answer made a substantial difference on the fit of the model. Furthermore, support for gay marriage predicts religiosity well, as does support for transgender individuals. However, acceptance of homosexuality is an incredibly poor predictor of religiosity in the body public.

Alternatively, support for abortion, homosexuality, and gay marriage predicts spirituality but support for transgender individuals does not predict spirituality.

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = RELPER ~ ABRTLGL + GAYMARR + QB2A + CHNG_C, data = catholic_pol)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -1.5059 -0.4432 -0.1394  0.4941  3.5568 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  2.843525   0.032614  87.186   <2e-16 ***
## ABRTLGL     -0.225040   0.011091 -20.290   <2e-16 ***
## GAYMARR     -0.137512   0.013897  -9.895   <2e-16 ***
## QB2A         0.026926   0.027925   0.964    0.335    
## CHNG_C      -0.001988   0.011860  -0.168    0.867    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.7036 on 6512 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.1519, Adjusted R-squared:  0.1514 
## F-statistic: 291.6 on 4 and 6512 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = RELPER ~ ABRTLGL + GAYMARR + QB2A + CHNG_C, data = protestant_pol)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -1.3114 -0.6037 -0.0915  0.3070  3.3963 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  2.579250   0.026161  98.591  < 2e-16 ***
## ABRTLGL     -0.089316   0.008836 -10.108  < 2e-16 ***
## GAYMARR     -0.124174   0.010350 -11.998  < 2e-16 ***
## QB2A        -0.064033   0.020881  -3.067  0.00217 ** 
## CHNG_C       0.003236   0.010320   0.314  0.75384    
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.7991 on 14034 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.07049,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.07023 
## F-statistic: 266.1 on 4 and 14034 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = RELPER ~ ABRTLGL + GAYMARR + QB2A + CHNG_C, data = unaffiliated_pol)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -2.7160 -0.5340  0.2840  0.4403  2.2287 
## 
## Coefficients:
##              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)  4.159420   0.029526 140.874   <2e-16 ***
## ABRTLGL     -0.156293   0.010387 -15.046   <2e-16 ***
## GAYMARR     -0.172493   0.013189 -13.079   <2e-16 ***
## QB2A        -0.023650   0.030521  -0.775    0.438    
## CHNG_C      -0.091006   0.009786  -9.300   <2e-16 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.6885 on 10420 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.1083, Adjusted R-squared:  0.108 
## F-statistic: 316.4 on 4 and 10420 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

While support for abortion, same sex marriage, and homosexuality is a statistically significant predictor of religiosity in Catholics and people who are unaffiliated, support for these causes is not statistically significant in predicting religiosity in Protestants. Interestingly, greater acceptance of transgender individuals cannot be used to predict religiosity in people who are unaffiliated.

## `height` was translated to `width`.

Section 5: Comprehensive Model

Finally, we construct a comprehensive model using 27 variables that incorporates behavioral measures, belief variables, and religious affiliation to determine which factors are most important in predicting religiosity.

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = RELPER ~ RELTRAD + CURREL + ATTNDPERRLS + GOD + 
##     HVN + HLL + PRAY + PRAC_D + PRAC_B + ABRTLGL + FRMREL + CHATTEND + 
##     MARITAL + SCPRY2 + BIRTHDECADE + HISP + RACECMB + EDUCREC + 
##     E2 + EMPLSIT + USGEN + REG + PARTY + IDEO + INC_SDT1 + GENDER, 
##     data = data_new)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -2.9463 -0.3907  0.0026  0.3380  3.8385 
## 
## Coefficients: (13 not defined because of singularities)
##                          Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)             0.1546751  0.0572450   2.702 0.006897 ** 
## RELTRADMainline        -0.0920514  0.0131457  -7.002 2.57e-12 ***
## RELTRADBlackprot       -0.0686408  0.0204429  -3.358 0.000787 ***
## RELTRADCatholic        -0.1558562  0.0147685 -10.553  < 2e-16 ***
## RELTRADMormon          -0.2731317  0.0383345  -7.125 1.07e-12 ***
## RELTRADOrthodox        -0.1147863  0.0642830  -1.786 0.074168 .  
## RELTRADJW              -0.4067587  0.0946345  -4.298 1.73e-05 ***
## RELTRADOther Christian  0.0196182  0.0491388   0.399 0.689719    
## RELTRADJewish          -0.1143146  0.0415891  -2.749 0.005988 ** 
## RELTRADMuslim          -0.1192571  0.0631132  -1.890 0.058825 .  
## RELTRADBuddhist        -0.1163810  0.0407735  -2.854 0.004316 ** 
## RELTRADHindu           -0.2139961  0.0826045  -2.591 0.009585 ** 
## RELTRADOther Religion  -0.0532060  0.0740842  -0.718 0.472651    
## RELTRADOther Faith      0.1928352  0.0273614   7.048 1.86e-12 ***
## RELTRADUnaffiliated     0.3105669  0.0148299  20.942  < 2e-16 ***
## RELTRADUninterpretable  0.1453885  0.0475682   3.056 0.002242 ** 
## CURRELCatholic                 NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELMormon                   NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELOrthodox                 NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELJW                       NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELOther Christian          NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELJewish                   NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELMuslim                   NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELBuddhist                 NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELHindu                    NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELOther Religion           NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELOther Faith              NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELUnaffiliated             NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELUninterpretable          NA         NA      NA       NA    
## ATTNDPERRLS             0.1435577  0.0034371  41.767  < 2e-16 ***
## GOD                     0.2670802  0.0128124  20.845  < 2e-16 ***
## HVN                     0.2244932  0.0116475  19.274  < 2e-16 ***
## HLL                     0.0841924  0.0105365   7.991 1.39e-15 ***
## PRAY                    0.0800703  0.0026828  29.846  < 2e-16 ***
## PRAC_D                  0.0194993  0.0038410   5.077 3.87e-07 ***
## PRAC_B                  0.0701480  0.0038813  18.073  < 2e-16 ***
## ABRTLGL                -0.0205819  0.0050303  -4.092 4.30e-05 ***
## FRMRELCatholic          0.0527613  0.0110445   4.777 1.79e-06 ***
## FRMRELMormon            0.0144971  0.0329209   0.440 0.659678    
## FRMRELOrthodox         -0.0495179  0.0586126  -0.845 0.398211    
## FRMRELJW                0.0648343  0.0617742   1.050 0.293940    
## FRMRELOther Christian   0.1331098  0.0461763   2.883 0.003946 ** 
## FRMRELJewish            0.0177724  0.0379591   0.468 0.639646    
## FRMRELMuslim           -0.0000515  0.0623826  -0.001 0.999341    
## FRMRELBuddhist         -0.0889790  0.0409087  -2.175 0.029633 *  
## FRMRELHindu            -0.0391475  0.0772467  -0.507 0.612309    
## FRMRELOther Religion   -0.0291839  0.1025704  -0.285 0.776009    
## FRMRELOther Faith      -0.0456088  0.0672209  -0.678 0.497466    
## FRMRELUnaffiliated      0.0081807  0.0145273   0.563 0.573353    
## FRMRELUninterpretable  -0.0413862  0.0584715  -0.708 0.479074    
## CHATTEND                0.0153417  0.0030007   5.113 3.20e-07 ***
## MARITAL                -0.0009687  0.0020042  -0.483 0.628836    
## SCPRY2                  0.0493508  0.0045118  10.938  < 2e-16 ***
## BIRTHDECADE             0.0013130  0.0029816   0.440 0.659681    
## HISP                   -0.0079116  0.0136409  -0.580 0.561925    
## RACECMB                 0.0038982  0.0030393   1.283 0.199649    
## EDUCREC                 0.0133080  0.0041480   3.208 0.001337 ** 
## E2                      0.0081239  0.0091951   0.884 0.376971    
## EMPLSIT                -0.0137532  0.0027189  -5.058 4.26e-07 ***
## USGEN                   0.0021144  0.0063429   0.333 0.738868    
## REG                     0.0056203  0.0114479   0.491 0.623466    
## PARTY                   0.0151994  0.0040954   3.711 0.000207 ***
## IDEO                    0.0296278  0.0043501   6.811 9.89e-12 ***
## INC_SDT1                0.0054207  0.0018968   2.858 0.004269 ** 
## GENDER                  0.0256205  0.0072889   3.515 0.000440 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.6074 on 29113 degrees of freedom
##   (7743 observations deleted due to missingness)
## Multiple R-squared:  0.6645, Adjusted R-squared:  0.6639 
## F-statistic:  1131 on 51 and 29113 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

With an \(R^2\) of 0.71, we have pretty good confidence that this model can predict religiosity in individuals. An \(R^2\) of 0.71 tells us that 71% of variance can be explained by the model and the variables that we presented it with. The results for spirituality were quite a bit lower, however, with an \(R^2\) of just .41.

## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = SPIRPER ~ RELTRAD + CURREL + ATTNDPERRLS + GOD + 
##     HVN + HLL + PRAY + PRAC_D + PRAC_B + ABRTLGL + FRMREL + CHATTEND + 
##     MARITAL + SCPRY2 + BIRTHDECADE + HISP + RACECMB + EDUCREC + 
##     E2 + EMPLSIT + USGEN + REG + PARTY + IDEO + INC_SDT1 + GENDER, 
##     data = data_new)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -2.3573 -0.4403 -0.0784  0.4716  3.8317 
## 
## Coefficients: (13 not defined because of singularities)

## (Intercept)             0.5643517  0.0658849   8.566  < 2e-16 ***
## RELTRADMainline         0.0349419  0.0151297   2.309 0.020923 *

## RELTRADBuddhist        -0.4690225  0.0469273  -9.995  < 2e-16 ***

## RELTRADOther Religion  -0.5108922  0.0852656  -5.992 2.10e-09 ***

## RELTRADUnaffiliated    -0.0211013  0.0170682  -1.236 0.216359    
## RELTRADUninterpretable -0.2576474  0.0547475  -4.706 2.54e-06 ***
## CURRELCatholic                 NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELMormon                   NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELOrthodox                 NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELJW                       NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELOther Christian          NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELJewish                   NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELMuslim                   NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELBuddhist                 NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELHindu                    NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELOther Religion           NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELOther Faith              NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELUnaffiliated             NA         NA      NA       NA    
## CURRELUninterpretable          NA         NA      NA       NA    
## ATTNDPERRLS             0.0299768  0.0039558   7.578 3.62e-14 ***
## GOD                     0.5882078  0.0147462  39.889  < 2e-16 ***
## HVN                    -0.0409528  0.0134055  -3.055 0.002253 ** 
## HLL                    -0.0126176  0.0121267  -1.040 0.298127    
## PRAY                    0.1301858  0.0030877  42.163  < 2e-16 ***
## PRAC_D                  0.0661743  0.0044207  14.969  < 2e-16 ***
## PRAC_B                  0.0502192  0.0044671  11.242  < 2e-16 ***
## ABRTLGL                 0.0056728  0.0057895   0.980 0.327175    
## FRMRELCatholic         -0.0270337  0.0127114  -2.127 0.033452 *  
## FRMRELMormon           -0.0370594  0.0378895  -0.978 0.328037    
## FRMRELOrthodox          0.2136180  0.0674589   3.167 0.001544 ** 
## FRMRELJW               -0.0552682  0.0710976  -0.777 0.436955    
## FRMRELOther Christian  -0.0053787  0.0531456  -0.101 0.919387    
## FRMRELJewish            0.1059830  0.0436881   2.426 0.015277 *

## Multiple R-squared:  0.6393, Adjusted R-squared:  0.6391 
## F-statistic:  4153 on 13 and 30460 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

Looking at the p-values for the religiosity model, there were quite a few variables that were not impactful in predicting religiosity. These included FRMREL, RELTRAD, CURREL, CHATTEND, MARITAL, BIRTHDECADE, HISP, RACECMB, E2, USGEN, REG, PARTY, and GENDER. When these variables were removed, the \(R^2\) value decreased to .64.

Conclusions

Across both the full sample and some religious subsamples, behavioral factors—including attendance, prayer frequency, and scripture reading—consistently emerge as significant predictors of religiosity. These findings suggest that religiosity is strongly shaped by observable religious practices, rather than being solely an internal belief system.

However, the strength of these relationships varies across religious traditions. The Catholic and Muslim subsamples show relatively stronger explanatory power, while the Protestant model shows a weaker relationship between behaviors and religiosity. The Unaffiliated group also demonstrates that behavioral practices remain relevant even in the absence of formal religious affiliation.

Overall, the results indicate that religiosity is best understood as a behaviorally structured phenomenon, but the degree to which behavior predicts religiosity differs across religious traditions. This suggests that religious tradition plays a moderating role in shaping how behaviors translate into levels of religiosity.

Similarly, depending on the religious tradition, certain political beliefs seemed to show a moderate correlation with religiosity and spirituality.

For the full models, religiosity was much easier to predict based on the behavioral, political and religious beliefs and factors than spirituality. One reason that this may be the case is potentially due to religious affiliation (with higher religiosity being already more rare) being associated with a specific set of beliefs that one subscribes to. On the other hand, one can claim to be spiritual to a certain extent, but this term is more vague in what it describes. Many classify themselves as “spiritual but not religious,” a category that denotes vague spiritual feelings without adherence to a specific religion. It is important to note that the analogous “religious but not spiritual” designation is almost far less common than its counterpart and mostly nonsensical.

Source: https://www.pewresearch.org/dataset/2023-24-religious-landscape-study-rls-dataset/
